focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

A few hours later Gavin woke to the sound of pipes gurgling in the walls. The kitchens beneath the Hufflepuff dormitories ran early on Christmas morning, and the warm-water pipes that cut through the stone behind his headboard ticked and knocked as they heated. It was the only sound. The other four beds in the room were empty except for Malcolm Preece's, and Malcolm was still asleep, his blankets pulled up to his eyebrows with one foot sticking out at the bottom, bare and pale.

There was snow against the window. It was not the charmed interior snow that sometimes drifted through the common room but real snow piled against the ground-level glass, dimming the light to a blue-grey wash. The fire had gone out overnight. Gavin's breath showed when he sat up.

At the foot of his bed were three things: his mother's parcel, wrapped in brown paper and tied with the same string she used to bundle newspapers for recycling, her handwriting neat and small on the tag; a package from Miren, soft, wrapped in pages torn from the Daily Prophet — she'd used the employment section, which was the only part that didn't carry war news these days; and a card from his aunt in the cream-coloured envelope she always used.

He opened his mother's parcel in bed, sitting cross-legged with the blankets over his shoulders. Inside was a hand-knitted dark blue jumper, too large as usual. She always knitted them too large because she was convinced he was growing faster than he was. There was also a tin of fudge with a note that said *Merry Christmas love Mum and Dad, the cat knocked over the tree again, Dad fixed it with a Sticking Charm so now we can't move it until June, we love you.* The fudge smelled like toffee and butter and the kitchen at home, and he ate a piece before he was fully...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he walls that he'd never noticed before this year.

Both of them had been kind. That made the loneliness worse. If they had laughed, or told him to stop being stupid, he could have gone upstairs angry. Instead he'd been handed tea and worry and a grip on his shaking hand, and none of it changed the fact that he was alone with it.

They were both wrong, too. He knew because the boundary pages had left a mark on him: when he let the memory of them back in, part of the common-room dark went flat and depthless, and the small everyday hum of magic in the room vanished there. His stomach dropped every time it happened. The outside did not think about him. It did not think at all. It was simply there.

He went to bed. In the dark he listened to his dormitory mates breathing in their sleep and found himself counting the separate rhythms, trying to tuck his own breath between them. They had enough to be frightened of already without this on top of it. Each time he shut his eyes, the dead center of the page came back behind them. Not as an image. As a place his thoughts skidded off and could not cross. The skin along his forearms tightened with cold, though the dormitory was stuffy with sleeping bodies. Somewhere above and beyond, the wards still held. Thinner than yesterday. Thinner than the day before that. Nobody else in the room knew it.

Gavin lay on his back and stared at the underside of his bed canopy and went over it again because there was nothing else to do. Nobody was going to help him. He was not good enough to do it alone. It was still going to be him, because there was nobody else and the manuscript was the only thing he'd found that even hinted at what to do. After a while he closed his eyes. He did not sleep. Much later, without noticing when it happened, he did.
